FEHB RETIREMENT

Federal Health Benefits in Retirement

FEHB is one of the best benefits you can take into retirement. We help you understand your options, coordinate with Medicare, and optimize your healthcare strategy.

CHAPTER I: FEHB FUNDAMENTALS

Your Health Benefits Bridge to Retirement

FEHB is one of the best benefits you can take into retirement — but only if you understand the rules, meet the requirements, and coordinate properly with Medicare. One mistake can cost you this valuable coverage forever.

Plan My FEHB Strategy

"FEHB plus Medicare gives you better coverage than most private retirees can buy."

— Mike Stevens, IAR

On This Page

  • Eligibility Requirements
  • 5-Year Rule
  • Medicare Coordination
  • Plan Selection
  • PSHB Changes
  • Optimization Strategy
CHAPTER II: ELIGIBILITY

Qualifying for FEHB in Retirement

Taking FEHB into retirement isn't automatic — you must meet specific requirements that many federal employees don't fully understand until it's too late. These rules are strictly enforced, and there are limited exceptions.

Two Critical Requirements

Immediate Annuity Retirement

You must retire on an immediate annuity — not deferred retirement. This means meeting one of FERS's immediate retirement eligibilities: MRA+30, age 60+20, or age 62+5.

5 Years Continuous Enrollment

You must be enrolled in FEHB for the 5 years immediately before retirement. This is 5 consecutive years — any gap in coverage can disqualify you.

72% Gov't Premium Contribution
5 Years Required Coverage
90% Fed Retirees Keep FEHB

If you meet these requirements, you can keep FEHB for life. The government continues to pay their share of the premium (approximately 72% of the weighted average), and you pay the same premium as active employees. This is an enormous benefit — private retirees often pay $1,500-$2,500 monthly for comparable coverage.

CHAPTER III: THE CRITICAL RULE

Understanding the 5-Year Rule

The 5-year rule is the most misunderstood and potentially costly FEHB requirement. It's not 5 total years of federal employment or 5 years of any health coverage — it's 5 consecutive years of FEHB enrollment immediately before retirement.

5-Year Rule Scenarios

Scenario FEHB Eligible? Explanation
Enrolled 5 straight years before retirement Yes Meets the requirement
Enrolled 8 years total, but gap in last 3 years No Must be continuous and immediate
Covered under spouse's FEHB for 2 years No Must be enrolled in your own name
Military deployment, returned to FEHB Maybe Limited exception may apply
Spouse loses federal job, you re-enroll Maybe Qualifying event may restart clock

Common 5-Year Rule Mistakes

  • Dropping FEHB to be covered under spouse's plan
  • Not re-enrolling immediately after military deployment
  • Assuming total years of federal service count
  • Not verifying enrollment status before retirement

Verify your FEHB enrollment history with HR at least 2 years before planned retirement to ensure compliance.

Limited exceptions exist for certain military service, but these are narrow and specific. The safest approach is to ensure you're enrolled in FEHB in your own name for the full 5 years before retirement — no gaps, no exceptions, no assumptions.

CHAPTER IV: MEDICARE INTEGRATION

FEHB + Medicare = Optimal Coverage

When you turn 65, enrolling in Medicare Parts A and B while keeping FEHB creates the gold standard of retiree health coverage. Medicare becomes primary, FEHB becomes secondary, and your combined coverage often exceeds what either provides alone.

A

Hospital Insurance

Premium-free for most federal retirees. Covers inpatient hospital care, skilled nursing, and hospice services.

B

Medical Insurance

Monthly premium required. Covers doctor visits, outpatient care, and medical supplies. Essential to avoid penalties.

D

Prescription Drug

Usually not needed if your FEHB plan has creditable drug coverage, which most do.

+

FEHB Secondary

Covers what Medicare doesn't, often eliminating deductibles and reducing copays significantly.

Coordination Benefits

With Medicare + FEHB

  • ✓ Minimal out-of-pocket costs
  • ✓ No deductibles on many plans
  • ✓ Comprehensive prescription coverage
  • ✓ Nationwide provider network

Without Medicare Coordination

  • × Higher FEHB deductibles
  • × Larger copays and coinsurance
  • × Medicare late enrollment penalties
  • × Reduced coverage options

Most FEHB plans offer enhanced benefits for Medicare enrollees. For example, many plans waive deductibles and reduce copays when Medicare pays first. The combination typically provides better coverage than Medicare plus a private Medigap policy, and often at lower total cost.

Critical timing: You have a 7-month initial enrollment period around your 65th birthday for Medicare Part B. Missing this window results in permanent late-enrollment penalties that increase your Part B premium for life.

CHAPTER V: PLAN OPTIMIZATION

Choosing the Right FEHB Plan

FEHB offers over 200 plan options, and your needs in retirement may differ significantly from your working years. Plans vary dramatically in their Medicare coordination benefits, provider networks, and out-of-pocket costs.

Retirement Plan Considerations

Factor Working Years Retirement Years Impact
Doctor Visits Occasional More frequent Copay amounts matter more
Prescription Drugs Minimal Likely increase Drug formularies critical
Medicare Coordination Not applicable Essential Plan-specific benefits
Provider Network Work location focused Home/retirement location May need to switch plans
Catastrophic Coverage Important Critical Out-of-pocket maximums

Many federal retirees benefit from switching to a plan with stronger Medicare coordination benefits, even if it means changing providers. The potential savings from enhanced coordination often outweigh the inconvenience of network changes.

200+ FEHB Plan Options
$2,400 Avg Annual Premium Savings
45 Days to Switch Plans
CHAPTER VI: POSTAL CHANGES

PSHB Program for Postal Employees

Starting in 2025-2026, postal employees and retirees were transitioned from FEHB to the new Postal Service Health Benefits (PSHB) program. This creates different rules and requirements for postal workers approaching retirement.

Key PSHB Differences

FEHB (Non-Postal)

  • 5-year enrollment rule
  • Medicare enrollment recommended
  • Over 200 plan options
  • Government contributes ~72%

PSHB (Postal Only)

  • 5-year enrollment rule applies
  • Medicare enrollment required
  • Fewer plan options, postal-specific
  • Postal Service contributes

Important for Postal Employees

If you're a postal employee or postal retiree, you're now in PSHB, not FEHB. The rules are similar but not identical. Medicare enrollment is required, not just recommended. Make sure you understand PSHB-specific requirements before retirement.

Postal employees who were eligible for FEHB in retirement were automatically transitioned to PSHB with similar benefits. However, new postal retirees must meet PSHB's requirements, which include mandatory Medicare enrollment when eligible.

CHAPTER VII: OPTIMIZATION

FEHB Retirement Strategy

Maximizing your FEHB benefits in retirement requires strategic planning that begins years before you retire. Small decisions made early can save thousands in healthcare costs over your retirement.

5-Year Retirement Timeline

5 Years Out
  • Verify FEHB enrollment status
  • Understand 5-year rule
  • Plan for no coverage gaps
3 Years Out
  • Research Medicare basics
  • Compare FEHB plans
  • Consider geographic moves
1 Year Out
  • Finalize FEHB plan choice
  • Schedule retirement planning
  • Prepare for Medicare decisions
Age 65
  • Enroll in Medicare A & B
  • Optimize plan coordination
  • Enjoy enhanced benefits

The most successful federal retirees treat FEHB planning as seriously as their TSP strategy. This benefit can provide $100,000+ in value over a 20-year retirement, making it worth careful planning and optimization.

Key insight: FEHB in retirement isn't just about keeping your health insurance — it's about creating a healthcare strategy that coordinates multiple programs (FEHB, Medicare, FSAs) to minimize costs and maximize coverage throughout your retirement years.

CHAPTER VIII: COMMON QUESTIONS

FEHB Planning FAQ

Everything you need to know about taking your federal health benefits into retirement.

Plan My FEHB Strategy

Yes, but you must meet two requirements: (1) You must retire on an immediate annuity (not a deferred retirement), and (2) You must be enrolled in FEHB for the 5 years immediately before retirement. There are limited exceptions to the 5-year rule for certain circumstances like returning from active military duty. If you meet these requirements, you can keep FEHB for life, and the government continues to pay approximately 72% of the weighted average premium just as they did while you were working.

Most federal retirees should enroll in Medicare Part A (which is premium-free if you have 40 quarters of Medicare-covered employment) and Part B when they turn 65. Medicare becomes your primary insurance and FEHB becomes secondary, which can significantly reduce your out-of-pocket costs. Some FEHB plans waive deductibles and copays for Medicare-enrolled members. Delaying Medicare Part B enrollment can result in permanent late-enrollment penalties. We recommend reviewing your specific FEHB plan's Medicare coordination benefits before deciding.

The 5-year rule requires continuous FEHB enrollment for the 5 years immediately preceding your retirement to carry FEHB into retirement. This means 5 consecutive years — not 5 total years. If you weren't enrolled in FEHB, were covered under a spouse's plan, or had a lapse in coverage during those last 5 years, you may not be eligible to continue FEHB. Limited exceptions exist for employees who return from active military service or lose coverage under a spouse's FEHB enrollment. It's critical to verify your eligibility well before your planned retirement date.

Once you enroll in Medicare, it becomes your primary payer and FEHB becomes secondary. This means Medicare pays first, then your FEHB plan pays some or all of what Medicare doesn't cover, depending on your specific plan's coordination rules. Many FEHB plans offer enhanced benefits for Medicare enrollees, such as waived deductibles or reduced copays. You continue paying your FEHB premium, but your combined coverage (Medicare + FEHB) is typically better than either alone. Most federal retirees find this combination provides excellent coverage without needing a Medicare Supplement (Medigap) policy.

The Postal Service Health Benefits (PSHB) program was created as a separate health insurance program for postal employees and retirees, distinct from FEHB. Starting in 2025-2026, postal employees and annuitants were transitioned from FEHB to PSHB. PSHB plans are similar to FEHB but are specifically designed for the postal workforce. Postal retirees in PSHB must enroll in Medicare Part A and Part B when eligible. If you're a postal employee approaching retirement, understanding the PSHB enrollment requirements and plan options is essential — the rules differ from traditional FEHB in important ways.
